reviews.review_text: old but gold, this is MUCH better than the so called "remastered" version made with UE5 slop. it has high quality in every way possible and is super fun unlike the remastered version.

I realized I never rated this game, so here I am
I have about 400 hours on steam, but I played more than that a long time ago (around 2008 I guess)
This game is fire. 
It has this sweet spot between the complexity and interesting quests of morrowind and the enjoyable gameplay (including fights) of skyrim. It still leaves you the ability to create your own spells (to break the game if you want).

I replayed it a few months ago with [b]Maskar's Oblivion Overhaul[/b], making the fights more enjoyable and the game more challenging overall, you can even build your own house wherever you want with it. it was amazing. 

The only thing I don't like much is the fact that you have to min max your character a bit if you don't want the enemies becoming (much) stronger than you as you level up, but this can be fixed with mods (eg: oblivion XP, set XP gained to X5 and don't train)
I especially recommend the dark brotherhood questline. Back when I was a teenager, I thought it was so cool. Now I think the voice acting of Lucien Lachance is hilarious. In both cases it was a blast.
I 100% recommend it.
MAKE THE GOTY DELUXE PURCHASABLE BUY ITSELF! I CANT EVEN GET IT BECAUSE I ALREADY OWN THE GOTY NON-DELUXE EDITION AND IT WONT LET ME PURCHASE IT "AGAIN".

I'm not a huge fan of the fantasy genre (despite the fact that I love FF) and a result these types of games normally don't resonate with me. But despite this I enjoyed my time with Oblivion, it's got a weird charm to it where it's so broken beyond beleif but in a funny, not annoying way. I played with mods that fixed things at one point and couldn't last 20 minutes but 8 hours of broken ♥♥♥♥ was actually pretty entertaining. I'm going to keep playing on my 360 to try and play an even more broken version.
Classic Elder Scrolls' games are always great to jump into again. Take some time to jump in to Tamriel and close shut the jaws of Oblivion
Very fun and classic and old school. one of the best all the time.

Just shut up and take my money Gabe...Dammit 

Only bug, and being honest enjoy having to quit the game to main screen and reload just for the intro every time, gets me right in the feels...

Audio bug/Linux Mint , worth it , I DONT CARE!!!!!!!
nostalgia kick from the past. so much creativity went into this game and is probably the best of the series
Peak childhood right here. I remember my brother getting this game an playing the hell outta this after school even though I never properly beat it!
when i was 10 i didnt understand nothing from this game, now replaying it, i still hit rats with sticks, gorgeous map tho

Oblivion was one of the 1st games I got when I bought my Steam Deck and I played it for at least  3 years now. In Oblivion we explore the world of Cyrodill during troubling times when the emperor was assassinated, and a cult called Mythic Dawn is scheming to destroy the world of Cyrodill with the help of the daedric lord of destruction. Despite its 2006 pixelated graphics the world of Cyrodill is beautiful.

During my in-game travels I often found myself stopping on the hills of Cyrodill to gaze upon the Imperial City in the distant, or some other gorgeous view that I stumbled upon. The combat of the game is pretty straightforward, especially if you already played any other game from the Elder Scrolls series like Skyrim or Morrowind. There's a good combination of weapons that you can use but which one you use will depend on what you selected as your major skill for levelling (Blade or Blunt weapons), or you can not go with either and choose to use magic or hand-to-hand combat since game also offers proficiency in that line of combat as well.

I think the levelling system in Oblivion works in the exact same way as it did in Morrowind (If I remember correctly). You can select about 8 Major Skills for your character, and levelling and becoming more proficient with these skills will also contribute to the level of your character, the rest non-selected skills become Minor Skills and although you can still level them up and receive their boons, these do not contribute to your character level. Each skill offers a boon at a quarter mark level to give you certain abilities that you can use that are associated with that skill.

So for example at level 25 Marksman you unlock an ability to zoom in with your bow for better accuracy, at level 50 you become a Journeyman of Marksman earning you another skill with a bow, eventually reaching the rank of a Master (level 100) which gives you a chance to paralyze a target with a bow. The world also has trainers scattered around the world for each skill and they can train you, and increase your skill level by 1 for money however, the rank of the teacher must be equal to or greater than your own (So for example a Journeyman Marksman trainer will not be able to train you anymore if you've become an Expert in Marksman).

The plot of the game itself is compelling and you meet interesting characters along the way however as it is with Elder Scrolls games, you spend most of your time doing side quests and exploring the world rather than the main quest, and the side quests in the game are pretty solid. I found the Dark Brotherhood quest line and the Thieves Guild questlines particularly interesting, while the line of quests associated with the Mad Isles quirky. Around the world there are also scattered shrines of Daedra lords which you can interact with and do a quest for each lord which will grant you a piece of special weapon or armor that you can either keep for yourself or return to the given lord.

After saving the city of Kvatch, the world will also be littered with Oblivion Gates which will take you to the realm of Oblivion where your objective becomes to climb a tower, and collect a special Seal which will trigger you being transported back to Cyrodill and the gate being closed. I would say that after you close somewhere between 10 - 20 gates, the remaining gates become pretty repetitive as the game recycles the layouts of the realms between the gates. From the reading that I have done, the unmodded game will provide at maximum 60 Gates for you to close and each time you close one, a new one appears somewhere randomly in the world (until you close 60 gates in total).

One of the things that I found a bit off-putting at first was the difficulty of the game. The difficulty of the game is configured in the settings through the use of a slider rather than the standard Easy, Normal, Hard keywords. By default the slider is set in the middle which I guess represents "Normal" difficulty but despite that I was still falling an easy pray to some of the enemies (especially groups of enemies). My advice is to not go anywhere near the city of Kvatch until you have completed a good bit of side quests, and levelled properly, otherwise you'll just get annihilated in that quest.

The biggest downside of the game, and the one that happened to me unfortunately is the save data corruption issue which basically caused me to abandon my adventure in Cyrodill as the last stable save I had was made last year, and I would lose pretty much hundred of hours of gameplay, if not more. For the record, I haven't added or attempted to add any mods to the game at any point, and I'm not sure how the corruption happened (although I did notice it first when I healed my horse with magic).

Basically it started with some of the textures/animations lagging in the air (like after using magic, the light generated from the spell would hang around my character forever when it was supposed to disappear shortly after using the spell), then I noticed that some of my saves would fail to load as they would be stuck in an infinite loading loop, similarly when I fast travelled to another place or simply tried to move between different areas the same infinite loading loop would also occur.

After doing some reading online, it appears that this save data corruption is pretty common even in the Remake of the game which came out last year, and in Skyrim (although I never experienced this issue with Skyrim when playing it on my PS3 and PS4). Basically to save yourself from losing your entire gameplay/hours of gameplay do these things.

- Disable the auto-save feature for all actions (when resting/sleeping, transporting between areas etc.)
- Keep between 3 - 5 manual saves around that you update regularly so you can roll back to one of them, should any of the other ones become corrupted.
- Don't install mods and if you do, don't uninstall them while the game is running, as this is the most common cause of corruption.

At 1st I wanted to give this game a negative review for the data save corruption as I was a bit salty (to say the least) about losing my whole gameplay since having such issues with modern games, and in these times is unacceptable. Upon reflection however, this game was made in 2006 (remaster in 2009) where such issues were still plaguing the gaming world so I will be a bit more forgiving in this case.

Overall I did after all enjoyed my adventure in Oblivion for as long as it lasted, the game provides you a beautiful open world that is filled to the brim with interesting locations, side quests, and characters that you can interact with. The game also runs pretty smoothly on Steam Deck LED as I haven't experienced any freezes and crashes were seldom. The game also doesn't consume a lot of power so you can play for hours without needing to charge.

Due to the mentioned corruption, more than likely I won't be coming back to this game. I am planning to play the Remake at some point in the future so perhaps I'll complete my adventure there one day. As long as you follow my advice I gave earlier to minimize the chances of data corruption, I'm sure you're going to find this game, and it's world appealing and fun.

Game was on sale and a friend suggested I buy it. I like the open-world and how bright and colorful it is compared to Skyrim. The main story is interesting too, Im excited to do the DLC's, other side quests and maybe try Morrowind next.
it has less bugs than the remaster, and somehow looks better than the remaster at times(piss filter vs real art direction) so I will recommend og oblivion

I understand it now, 16 hours in and haven't even touch a single main quest yet. Some of the faction quests are suprisingly fun and pretty engaging in my opinion, very old school like. Im not a huge fan of the leveling system but somehow it makes the progression feels more rewarding. The lore and world building is the winner here honestly, and i get why it got so much love from the community. Combat? Bethesda.

This is the first elder scrolls game that I have actually put any time to and I am hooked. Even though this game is 20 years old the quests and the depth of the openworld is better than most games that are released today.
more mystical and whimsical than skyrim, but better...? Up to interpretation

Even though cyrodil is alot more basic and flat than skyrim and vvandenfell they still created a amazing gem of an open world visually. Despite how old the game is there a uniquely colorful waterpainting look to the games forests. There is a bit of magical fatastical look compared to the other games. It contrasts alot of the oblivion gates and deadlands design. Even though the dungeons there are quite reptitive the oblivion dungeon in kvatch is quite well designed and the destroyed city is a insane contrast to others. Progressing the main quest really changes the atmosphere of every around the other cities too. 

Each city also has such a beutiful design. Bruma's snowy lodge cabin look. The white ayleid look of the imperial city. The gothic look of skingrad and chedinyhal. The chipped paints and colorful houses of lewwyin and bravil also shares the dirty marshier look with lewwyin. Most of the city also have a pretty intuitive uniform layout that says alot about the imperial culture with guild halls, shops, residents, chapel, and a keep with a count. Each city also has large amount of another race unique to that city. Bravil has khajit and argonians, Lewwyin has argonians and a torture chamber for them, Burma has nords, Cheydinhal has a dark elf count and retired orcs, and anvil has redguards. The imperial city large for a bethesda game and is alot nicer to traverse than vivec or mournhold in morrowind. 

The attributes system from morrowind is still in this game and they gave you fast travel to every city at the beinging of the game which is a bad change. They removed a few skills, niche wepon types, and sadly traversal spells like fly and teleport since the cities are only rendered after opening a loading screen. Progress is alot worse since they level scale everything in this game your chacters can fall behind if you dont get the most out of level ups. Which will require that you skill up 10 skill ups related to an atribute but the problem is 10 major skill ups level ups so if you have too many major skills related to your classes main attributes you will gain less every level up and level up too quickly. Its the exact same in morrowind but its less punishing because level scaling is minmal

Despite what many say a high skill high level chacter does play alot different than low level ones. Melee builds rely alot of fatigue and knockback. Mages can use weakness to magic, paralysis, and drain fatigue to make enemies helpless. fortification spells healing magic resistance and summoning also make mage builds have high survivability. Sleath builds are kinda weak in this game too but chamleon enchanted items and invisibility potions make it possible to get multiple sneak attacks. Almost every build is boosted by potions and enchanting. Being able to double an attribute then boost more with spells and items allows for alot of insanity. 100% magic resistance and chamleon invisbility is suprisingly easy and broken. The game is a real sand box at higher levels. Unlike morrowind you can easily stack weakness to magic which is the most broken spell in the game. Cast it onyourself or other and it doubles the effect including weakness to magic again. So you can 4x a damage spell or boost. Boost so powerful you can run across the map in 2 minutes

The quest design is awesome in this game. The scope and creativity of the faction and side quests are quite fun. It is a shame that they removed interfactional conflict but the quests are each faction is really good. The mages guild does not really force the player to enage with it mechanics which is a real loss. Meleeing the enemies to death and mage enemies being kind of weak really hurts inviting the use the strongest mechanics the game offers. Though it is crazy that the shivering isles is another questline for the game. Adding a whole madness island on top of the current open world is very unique compared.

Being a more recent fan of the Elder Scrolls franchise, the first Elder Scrolls game I was familiar with was Skyrim. However, I was curious about the lore within the rest of the series and how the gameplay differed. So, naturally, I moved straight into Oblivion, and I do not regret it. The gameplay is familiar enough that I don't feel lost, and I love the mechanics possibly more than I like some of the mechanics of Skyrim. Still a very unseasoned gamer here, but I can really appreciate the game quality (despite it being a decently aged game) and I especially love the mechanics of the classes and the options for more personalized customization with the player build. Overall, I really like it so far and would recommend.

First of all, this game will grow on you, so give it a little time. Even though the graphics are pretty outdated in 2026, there are moments where I have to stop and just admire the environment. Coming from Skyrim to this is absolutely fascinating, seeing all the changes, what they improved and what they maybe should have kept. (Like portable alchemy tools!?) For a Bethesda game, it's incredibly stable. I've encountered maybe one or two bugs. We'll see if that changes when I head into the expansion content.

Great game. Feeling of the progression is great to the point that in late game you mostly fly around. There are good enough ways to mess with the game. The difficulty is rather discrete because certain new enemies appear when you reach certain level and initially they can be tough. It's way better than insane random difficulty of daggerfall that you may encounter an insanely tough enemy when you are level 1. Also I think it's better than difficulty curve of skyrim as you are almost always weaker than enemies. Even if you don't optimize your level progression, typically it's not unfair with default settings (but it can get hectic under rare conditions).
Classic Elder Scrolls gameplay. Open world, lots of quests and NPCs. Always great to revisit Tamriel.
A great game, even by today's standards. It runs on my crappy laptop, so I can take this game with me wherever I go, 10/10

I made a lot of attempted playthroughs of this game but never really sunk my teeth into it, and I certainly have no interest in the remaster. That said, in an era where remasters are used as an excuse to delist the originals it's refreshing to see this game still available. I don't know how much I want to hand it to Bethesda given that shadow unlisting the original would've seriously soured the hype that carried the shadow drop strategy for the remaster, but it's nice to see even if it's not bucking the overall trend.

I spent so much time on this game on console (360) and finished it 10x over. It's a very mid-late 2000's Bethesda title, got its bugs (features) and whatnot, but still a solid playthrough experience, especially if you're going for that era's graphics aesthetic. The remaster is just as good with better visuals, but the original's aesthetics and graphics are still beautiful for their time.

but if you put aside the flaws of Oblivion you can have a great time and enjoy this memes game with a great ambiance and soundtrack(one of the best for me)
the principal story is cool but where oblivion shine is the guildes and the substory.

this game is kinda a nostalgia trip and the old mechanique can be... outdated but its still worth to play,
and if you have the money you still can play the remmasterd  version who is much eazier to play overall.
it's my first time playing, but it's a thoroughly excellent game and i still feel like i have loads to do even 46 hours in. and that's entirely without any mods!

The nostalgia alone is intoxicating, like the verbally auditory vibrancy that catches you off-guard. And when you see it has many other iterations, your brain sometimes whines about fairness because you found this gem so late,..,

Played for a hundred hours and still far from the end. A good game that aged like wine and will make you happy for weeks and weeks. Of course, there are some problems with bugs and translation, but most of them can be solved using mods that are quite easy to put in. Buy, play, and be happy.
great game, graphics are outdated, but mods will fix that problem but combat kinda sucks but overall fun quirky game

It's more that 20 years old, yet it's still one of the best RPG games you can play today.
It has it's flaws, but it has exceeded my expectations.
awesome childhood game refreshed, though remastered version is there now but this one remains in our hearts forever........

Great game! Quests don't just constantly send you to dungeons or caves but have you in the world doing things that the npcs will talk about. The green overworld contrasting with the oblivion gates lava hell scapes is an appreciated choice. Definitely the game I would mark as the beginning of Bethesda getting lazy with the incomplete areas and factions that never got patched even with the DLC packs that came out. Good thing they didn't have proc gen quests yet for this! Leveling is the main issue people complain about with this game, but really it's just balanced to punish power leveling because the game would be way too easy if it didn't. Don't worry about efficient leveling, and don't spam level things instead of playing the game. Enjoy this for what it is, a strange world where everyone is trying to cope with the fact that the forces of another world are invading to destroy them.

Good 'ol addicting Scrolls gameplay with fun, quirky NPCs. All sidequests are very good. Main story is fine, but not the main draw of the game. Also, Sir Patrick Stewart and Sean Bean are in it, so that's a big plus.

If you enjoy TES V: Skyrim, Tainted Grail: TFofA, Dread Delusion, TES III: Morrowind, KC: Deliverance I and II, Witcher and Witcher-likes, King's Field-likes etc. There'll be something here that you'll 100% enjoy.

You'll need but a handful of simple mods to get the game running smooth and bug-free. Goes on sale quite often so price shouldn't be an issue. Enjoy.

^ About my playtime: I played this game WAY more on console, and recently bought it on Steam

Oblivion is one of my favorite games of all time.
It has in-depth lore and incredibly unique dialogue,
the art style, which may be jarring for some at first, is/becomes incredibly charming.
Because of its age, Oblivion does have some issues, for example, there are many issues that require mods to fix (e.g.: Unofficial Oblivion Patch, A-Bomb, etc.)
With a few mods to patch the issues, the game runs incredibly well and still offers a wonderful experience

It's one of those games that you want to erase your memory and play them all over again for the first time. 
For me, it's also one of those games that when I see them in my library, I almost get teary-eyed because of how INCREDIBLE the game is, the feelings that it invokes..

Just unique in its own way. Definitely a must play at least once in a life time.

I don’t know what’s going on half the time but cool knights and spells! Love the open world here and the graphics look… okay for the most part. The character creation in this game is SO cool but I do wish it was a little more in depth with the appearance side. Overall fun game to mindlessly wonder a medieval world with

Still great fun. I think I enjoyed it more this time around than I did 20 years ago, as back then I was comparing it (unfavourably) to Morrowind. This time around I could just enjoy it for what it is, and mostly had an excellent time :)
For context on hours played, the playthrough at 44 hours has consisted of the Main Quest Line, The Fighters Guild and the Arena, so still many hours of fun ahead... especially if I allow my Champion to join the Dark Brotherhood! :0

Still a masterpiece in 2026. Oblivion remains one of the best RPGs ever made. The world is huge and alive, the quests are memorable (and often hilarious), and the freedom to play however you want is still unmatched by most modern games. Character building, exploration, and emergent chaos are all top-tier.

Pros:
Still incredibly fun and deep, doesn’t hold your hand too much or oversimplify everything.
Excellent mod support. A few quality-of-life and graphics mods make it feel fresh.
Proper DLCs you actually own, no shop nonsense or battle passes.
Runs great once set up.

Cons:
Looks dated compared to the remaster (easily fixed with mods).
Takes some initial fiddling to run smoothly on Windows 10/11.

If you want the authentic Oblivion experience that defined a generation, the classic version is still fantastic. Install the community patches and a few essential mods and you’ll have one of the most enjoyable RPGs ever created.

Highly recommended. Just push through the first-hour setup, It's more than worth it.
morrow wind,oblivion,skyrim,fallout 3 and fallout 4 since they came out on whatever machine i was using now its steamdeck and steam on pc

“A beautifully weird, endlessly charming RPG that still feels magical.”

The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion is one of those games that doesn’t just age—it develops a personality. Sometimes awkward, sometimes hilarious, but always full of life and imagination.

The world of Cyrodiil is still incredibly fun to explore. From lush forests and ancient ruins to the chaotic Oblivion gates, there’s always something waiting just around the corner. The game rewards curiosity in a very classic Bethesda way: wander off the path and you’ll probably find a story worth remembering.

What really gives Oblivion its identity is its NPC behavior and dialogue. Yes, it’s janky. Yes, it’s meme-worthy. But that unpredictability also makes the world feel strangely alive. Conversations can shift from serious fantasy drama to absolute absurdity in seconds, and somehow that becomes part of the charm.

The guild questlines are still some of the best Bethesda has ever made. The Dark Brotherhood, Thieves Guild, and Mage’s Guild especially stand out with strong storytelling and memorable missions that often outshine the main quest.

Combat and leveling, however, show their age. The scaling system can feel awkward, and encounters sometimes become more about stats than strategy. It’s functional, but not as refined as later entries in the series.

Pros:

Extremely immersive open world
Fantastic guild questlines
Tons of freedom and exploration
Unintentionally hilarious NPC interactions

Cons:

Dated combat and leveling system
Enemy scaling can feel inconsistent
Some mechanics feel rough by modern standards

Verdict:
Oblivion is messy, charming, and unforgettable. It may not be the most polished Elder Scrolls game, but it might be the most personality-filled one.

Recommended 👍

This one is as green as Fallout 3 (hey, Todd! Mirrakill.). Thou, I enjoyed it much more than Skyrim and F3, I can't say I'd recommend it. You're better off watching some video essays about this game and never touch it.

so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ boring. Completely empty "open world". Everyone said "It's so much better than Skyrim, the quests are so good!!" but it's exactly the same. Same ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ combat and overall gameplay loop. Same lack of actual RPG elements and implementation of such into the gameplay. Same terrible quest writing. It's just a proto-Skyrim. I don't know how the hell Bethesda won multiple GOTY awards with this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ of a franchise, but I hope it ends completely. I hope ES6 never comes out. I hope Bethesda goes completely bankrupt so I can enjoy peace and quiet and never have to hear anything about their ♥♥♥♥♥♥ games and how "cUlTuRaLlY iMpOrTaNt" they are for video games as a whole.

Love the charm, love the custom classes, love the colors and characters. Apparently the guilds are awesome.
runs pretty smoothly has very fun quests but the only thing i dont like is how slow you level up
very fun and i dont know how to explain the game you just gotta play it
Better than the remake imho. I do love both, but man, nothing beats the voice acting of the original. Quirky, buggy, beautiful, and nostalgic as hell. Cannot recommend enough.

I'm tired of Bethesda games. The only way to make them playable for a majority of their players is mods. Not only do the mods bring more life to their games, but of course fix the plethora of bugs they can never seem to fix themselves. And of course, the mods bring more and more players into the fold. The past 5 years, it seems as if Bethesda not only wants to rip off those same modders, they want to make it even harder for them to do the thing they've dedicated years, if not decades, to that which they love.

I gave up on Blizzard when it was horribly apparent they considered their players nothing but walking wallets, and now, Bethesda has finally opened my eyes to them. Combined I have 7k+ hours to their games, from this point on, not another minute more.
STOP RIGHT THERE, CRIMINAL SCUM! You have broken the law, all your stolen goods are now forfeit!

- Start game, small menu tells me to modify my setting if I want to
- Launch game, none of the settings I chose applied, so I have to re-apply them in-game and restart
- Start actual game, screen completely black
- Have to go through the entire settings AGAIN just to find what causes this
- Relaunch game, controls must have been mapped by a Neanderthal
- Want to change keybinds, game crashes

When your ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ game subjects me to something like this in the first ten minutes then I'm sorry, but I don't want to have anything to do with it. And to think I was upset with Half-Life 2 when I got one game-stopping bug on one of my repeat playthroughs.

Wow. Just... Wow. It's probably one of the best RPGs I've ever played; and I rarely play RPGs.

(At the time of writing, I am 8 hrs into Oblivion; and I'm looking forward to put more hours into it.)
